title: Missions & Establishments
subtitle: The 21 missions, their sub-stations, the Russian colony, and New Helvetia, 1769–1846
abstract: Every fixed establishment of colonial California on one map: the 21 Franciscan missions with founding years, the asistencias and estancias that extended them inland, a selected layer of the missions' named ranchos and outstations (Suisun and Napa for Sonoma, San Mateo for Dolores, Temecula and San Jacinto for San Luis Rey, and more), the Russian-American Company's fort, port, and farm ranches on the Sonoma coast, and Sutter's New Helvetia on the Sacramento. The outstation list is selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos. Use the timeline to watch the chain grow north and the rivals appear. Outstation and ranch locations are approximate; mission sites are exact.

features.name: Mission San Diego de Alcalá
features.summary: First of the chain; destroyed in the 1775 revolt and rebuilt at this valley site.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Carlos Borromeo (Carmel)
features.summary: Serra's headquarters and burial place; moved from Monterey to Carmel in 1771.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Antonio de Padua
features.summary: The upper Salinas valley mission, in Salinan country.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Gabriel Arcángel
features.summary: The richest of the southern missions; Toypurina's 1785 revolt; the gateway of the overland routes.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Luis Obispo de Tolosa
features.summary: Founded on the strength of the Portolá expedition's bear hunts in the Cañada de los Osos.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Francisco de Asís (Dolores)
features.summary: The Bay's first mission; its neophyte flights across the water drove the 1795–97 East Bay expeditions.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Juan Capistrano
features.summary: Founded twice, 1775 and 1776, after the San Diego revolt interrupted the first attempt.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission Santa Clara de Asís
features.summary: Paired with the pueblo of San José, whose fields bordered too closely for the friars' comfort.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Buenaventura
features.summary: The Channel mission Serra founded last in person; armed with cannon in the 1798 census.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission Santa Bárbara
features.summary: The 'Queen of the Missions,' beside the presidio; a center of the 1824 Chumash revolt.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission La Purísima Concepción
features.summary: Seized by Chumash rebels for a month in 1824; rebuilt after the 1812 earthquake at the present site.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission Santa Cruz
features.summary: Across the river from the villa of Branciforte, whose settlers the friars blamed for most of their troubles.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission Nuestra Señora de la Soledad
features.summary: The lonely Salinas valley mission; two governors died within its walls.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San José
features.summary: Base of the East Bay and Delta frontier; Estanislao was its alcalde before his 1829 revolt.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Juan Bautista
features.summary: Sited at 'San Benito' per Lasuén's 1796 reconnaissance synthesis (C-A 50); the 1798 Talholostl battle was fought nearby.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Miguel Arcángel
features.summary: Sited at 'las Pozas' in the same 1795–96 siting campaign.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Fernando Rey
features.summary: The valley mission on the LA road; armed with 2-pounders in the 1805 census.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Luis Rey de Francia
features.summary: Founded 13 Jun 1798, 54 children baptized that day (C-A 24 Doc 277); the largest mission population; Duflot drew its plan in the gallery.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission Santa Inés
features.summary: The last Channel mission; the 1824 revolt began here.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Rafael Arcángel
features.summary: Begun as a hospital asistencia of Dolores, raised to full mission in 1822; the northern frontier's answer to sickness and to Ross.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Mission San Francisco Solano (Sonoma)
features.summary: The last and northernmost mission, founded under Mexican rule partly to face the Russians; seed of Vallejo's Sonoma.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Santa Margarita de Cortona (asistencia)
features.summary: Grain-station and chapel over the cuesta from San Luis Obispo; the rancho kept the name. Sub-station of Mission San Luis Obispo.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: San Antonio de Pala (asistencia)
features.summary: The inland asistencia of San Luis Rey; uniquely, still a functioning mission chapel serving a Native community today. Sub-station of Mission San Luis Rey.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Las Flores (asistencia)
features.summary: The coast station between San Luis Rey and San Juan Capistrano; later the Pico brothers' rancho. Sub-station of Mission San Luis Rey.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Santa Ysabel (asistencia)
features.summary: San Diego's mountain asistencia in Kumeyaay country; the same district as the register's Santa Isabel fight of 1826. Sub-station of Mission San Diego.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: San Bernardino de Sena (estancia)
features.summary: San Gabriel's estancia at the foot of the pass; the rancho outpost end of the mission system, in the raiding corridor. Sub-station of Mission San Gabriel.

sources.citation: Founding dates per the standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal.; Engelhardt).

features.name: Colony Ross
features.summary: The Russian-American Company's fort and village on Kashaya land, 1812 to 1841. The Presidial System map carries its strength series; Vallejo's 1833 reconnaissance (C-A 53 Doc 45) describes it in full.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

features.name: Port Rumiantsev (Bodega)
features.summary: The company's anchorage and warehouses at Bodega Bay, in use from about 1809. The port the Spanish discovered in 1775, working under a Russian name.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

features.notes: Anchorage in Bodega Harbor; the precise landing/port site is approximate.

features.name: Kostromitinov Ranch
features.summary: Company farm on the Russian River plain, one of the three ranches feeding Ross in its last decade. Location approximate.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

features.name: Khlebnikov Ranch
features.summary: Company farm inland from Bodega, named for the RAC agent. Location approximate.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

features.name: Chernykh Ranch
features.summary: The agronomist Chernykh's farm; Voznesensky sketched it in 1841 (the sketch is in this site's gallery). Location approximate.

sources.citation: Russian establishments per the RAC literature (Gibson; Istomin) and the Spanish-Mexican reconnaissance record (C-A 12, C-A 53).

features.name: Suisun outstation (Solano)
features.summary: The Sonoma mission's grazing outstation in the Suisun Valley, at the Rockville and Cordelia end rather than the later town of Fairfield; in the country of the Suisun Patwin, and absorbed into the Vallejo-era ranchos after secularization. Outstation of Mission San Francisco Solano.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: Napa outstation (Solano)
features.summary: The mission's station in the lower Napa Valley, likewise folded into private ranchos in the 1830s. Outstation of Mission San Francisco Solano.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: San Pedro y San Pablo (San Mateo)
features.summary: Mission Dolores' farm outstation on the peninsula, established in the 1780s; the 1830 SF padron still lists San Mateo among the jurisdiction's ranchos (C-A 50 Doc 425). Outstation of Mission San Francisco de Asís.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: Rancho San Francisco Xavier
features.summary: San Fernando's northern stock rancho in the Santa Clarita valley; the same Rancho San Francisco where 18 Indians were killed in 1846 (military register #117). Outstation of Mission San Fernando.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: Santa Ysabel rancho (San Miguel)
features.summary: One of San Miguel's named ranchos with an adobe, up the Salinas from the mission. Outstation of Mission San Miguel.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: San Simeon rancho (San Miguel)
features.summary: San Miguel's coast station at San Simeon bay. Outstation of Mission San Miguel.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: Temecula station (San Luis Rey)
features.summary: San Luis Rey's inland station among the Luiseno communities of the Temecula valley. Outstation of Mission San Luis Rey.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: San Jacinto rancho (San Luis Rey)
features.summary: The mission's easternmost stock rancho, toward the pass country. Outstation of Mission San Luis Rey.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: San Gorgonio estancia (San Gabriel)
features.summary: San Gabriel's furthest estancia, in the pass between the mountains; the raiding corridor's doorstep. Outstation of Mission San Gabriel.

sources.citation: Mission-estate outstations per the standard accounts (Engelhardt; county histories); locations approximate. Selected, not exhaustive: the missions ran many more ranchos.

features.notes: Verify against the 1834-36 secularization inventories (C-A 50-53) for the documented estate lists.

features.name: Sutter's Fort (New Helvetia)
features.summary: Sutter's Mexican land-grant colony at the Sacramento-American confluence, founded 1839. Buyer of Ross's movable property in 1841, magnet for the overland emigrants, and the hinge between the mission-rancho world and the American conquest. The anti-Micheltorena revolt charged the governor with his 'relaciones secretas' with Sutter (C-A 61).

sources.citation: Standard accounts (Bancroft, Hist. Cal., IV); cf. Leg. Rec. (C-A 61) Doc 19
